flashcards.back: A specialized computing system designed to perform specific, dedicated functions within larger mechanical or electrical systems. It is optimized for particular tasks with real-time constraints, power limitations, and cost considerations.

flashcards.front: List the three main hardware components of an embedded system
flashcards.back: 1) Processor (CPU/Microcontroller/Microprocessor), 2) Memory (ROM, RAM, Flash), 3) Input/Output devices (sensors, actuators, communication interfaces)

flashcards.front: Classify embedded systems based on performance and complexity
flashcards.back:
1) Small-scale: 8-bit processors, limited resources
2) Medium-scale: 16-bit processors, moderate resources
3) Sophisticated: 32-bit/64-bit processors, complex OS, high resources

flashcards.front: What are the key characteristics of embedded systems?
flashcards.back: 1) Dedicated functionality, 2) Real-time operation, 3) Low power consumption, 4) Cost-effective, 5) Reliability & stability, 6) Minimal user interface, 7) Deterministic behavior
Microcontroller: Integrated CPU + memory + I/O on single chip (self-contained). Microprocessor: CPU only, requires external memory and I/O (component-level design).
An integrated circuit that combines all components of a computer system (CPU, memory, I/O, sometimes radio) on a single chip for embedded applications.

flashcards.front: Define deterministic behavior in embedded systems
flashcards.back: The system guarantees a response within a known worst-case time limit. The timing of operations is predictable and repeatable, crucial for real-time systems.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between hard and soft real-time systems?
flashcards.back: Hard real-time: Missing deadline = system failure (e.g., airbag control). Soft real-time: Missing deadline degrades performance but not fatal (e.g., video streaming).

flashcards.front: List common input devices in embedded systems
flashcards.back: Sensors (temperature, pressure, motion), switches, keypads, ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converters), encoders, touchscreens.

flashcards.front: List common output devices in embedded systems
flashcards.back: LEDs, LCD/LED displays, actuators (motors, solenoids), DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converters), relays, speakers.

flashcards.front: What is watchdog timer in embedded systems?
flashcards.back: A hardware timer that resets the system if software fails to respond within a specified time period, providing fault tolerance and system recovery.

flashcards.front: Explain memory hierarchy in embedded systems
flashcards.back:
1) Register: Fastest, inside CPU
2) Cache: Fast, between CPU and RAM
3) RAM: Main working memory (volatile)
4) ROM/Flash: Program storage (non-volatile)

flashcards.front: What is the difference between volatile and non-volatile memory?
flashcards.back: Volatile memory (RAM): Loses data when power is off. Non-volatile memory (ROM, Flash, EEPROM): Retains data without power. Used for program storage.
Permanent software programmed into non-volatile memory (ROM/Flash) that provides low-level control for hardware. It is typically smaller and more specialized than application software.

flashcards.front: What are the performance metrics for embedded systems?
flashcards.back: 1) Execution time, 2) Power consumption, 3) Code size, 4) Memory usage, 5) Response time (latency), 6) Throughput, 7) Reliability (MTBF)
